What's going on everybody? Dr. Kashi and Pupzilla here to give you a quick rundown on why I think hairdressers put experts out of business every day. And this all kind of begins with, you know, we've all spent tons of years of our lives educating ourselves to become experts in our fields. There's a ton of focus on shiny new methods, cutting edge science, precise protocols, yada, yada, yada, yet something is missing. And I fear it leads to the bane of every expert's existence. So it goes like this, have you ever been frustrated that a person will take advice from a hairdresser or bartender or whatever, but manage to ignore you, an expert in the subject? I just want to let you know that your frustration is shared with countless other professionals. And as a reasonable professional, it's logical to take advice from hairdressers. So long as it's advice about hairdressing, right? For another reason, the boundaries of those practices, they get fuzzy and they get lost every day. And I'll tell you why. It's the secret. I feel like every professional in really any field needs to know and it's been hiding in plain sight forever. And the hairdresser phenomenon, as I like to call it, is because of rapport. It's because of relationship development. It's because of trust. That trust is not built with positioning. The positioning is built with trust. And this is exactly why the hairdresser phenomenon is so prevalent and influential. This trust is built with a high frequency of positive contact points. In plain English, if you give a damn about a person, they will give a damn about you right back. Practically speaking, that means their adherence to a plan that you give them, the weight of the advice you give them, it all increases as their trust and respect in you as a person. And the key to developing this trust and respect in a systematic way is to develop a communication cycle with the people you work with. This goes in business, if you have clients, if you're a consultant, it really doesn't matter. I don't think family, right? Spouse really doesn't matter. Communication cycles are the most important. And keeping them engaged while also letting them know you give a damn. And so on the client side of things, a systematic onboarding process is an answer to this problem. It is important to invest and continue to pour energy into developing and scaling any rapport building process, because that's effectively what this is. This is accomplished by understanding and implementing the true nature of being a practitioner or coach rather than just a run of the mill programmer. Are you just feeding people directions and then hoping for the best? Or are you easing a person? Are you allowing vulnerability on both sides? Are you emotionally attuned to that person so that they trust the information you're giving them and that they are open and willing to accept the advice you give? And at the same time, you are open and willing to accept feedback and manipulate the program that you gave them to fit the client's needs or family member or spouse or team member or whatever, right? This all falls into the same stuff. Are you implementing reliable communication? You allow yourself more opportunity to develop all the trust you need as you go. So essentially, if you give a damn now, you front load all of that effort, it's more business now for more BSing later, right? I hope you guys found some value in that. It was a lesson that was hard learned for me and I still put energy into making it better every day.
